h1Title: Acute Appendicitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute appendicitis is inflammation of the vermiform appendix and the most common abdominal surgical emergency worldwide, affecting approximately 8-9% of individuals during their lifetime. Peak incidence occurs between ages 10 and 30, with approximately 250,000 appendectomies performed annually in the United States. The condition develops when the appendiceal lumen becomes obstructed — by a fecalith, lymphoid hyperplasia, or rarely a tumor — leading to bacterial overgrowth, ischemia, and progressive inflammation. If untreated, the appendix perforates within 24-72 hours in most cases, releasing bowel contents into the peritoneal cavity and causing life-threatening peritonitis. Laparoscopic appendectomy cures uncomplicated appendicitis with a complication rate below 5% and hospital stay of 1-2 days.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime risk approximately 8-9%; annual incidence 100-130 per 100,000 population in the US; approximately 300,000 appendectomies performed annually in the US alone
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence ages 10-30 years; male-to-female ratio approximately 1.4:1 overall; women aged 20-40 have higher rates of negative appendectomy due to overlapping gynecological pathology
keyStats.avgAge: Most common in second and third decades of life; rare before age 2; incidence declines after age 40
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 7-8 million cases annually worldwide; a leading cause of emergency surgery in all global regions

definition: Acute appendicitis (ICD-10: K35.80) is acute inflammation of the vermiform appendix, a blind-ended tubular structure approximately 6-10 cm in length originating from the posteromedial aspect of the cecum at the convergence of the taeniae coli. Obstruction of the appendiceal lumen — most commonly by a fecalith (inspissated feces, in 30-35% of cases) or by reactive lymphoid hyperplasia (in younger patients, typically following viral enteritis) — raises intraluminal pressure, disrupts mucosal barrier integrity, and allows luminal bacteria to invade the appendiceal wall. Progressive transmural inflammation, vascular thrombosis, and gangrenous necrosis follow within 12-24 hours; perforation typically occurs at 24-72 hours after symptom onset. Pathologically, appendicitis is classified as simple (non-perforated, non-gangrenous), gangrenous, or perforated (complicated). The Alvarado score and Appendicitis Inflammatory Response (AIR) score are validated clinical decision tools combining history, physical examination, and laboratory findings to stratify risk and guide imaging. Appendicitis is primarily managed by general surgeons, with gastroenterologists involved in diagnosis and in non-operative management protocols.

typesClassification.description: Acute inflammation limited to the appendiceal wall without gangrene, perforation, or pericecal abscess. The majority of cases on presentation. Treatable by laparoscopic appendectomy with excellent outcomes; non-operative antibiotic management is an established alternative for carefully selected patients.

Full-thickness necrosis of the appendiceal wall from vascular thrombosis and ischemia, preceding perforation. Associated with higher wound infection rates and increased risk of stump complications. Requires prompt surgery; antibiotic-only management has higher failure rates than simple appendicitis.

Free perforation releasing enteric bacteria into the peritoneal cavity; causes generalized purulent or feculent peritonitis, systemic sepsis, and paralytic ileus. Surgical mortality increases sharply — overall perforation rates are approximately 20-30% of all appendicitis cases, higher in children under 5 and elderly patients.

Localized perforation walled off by omentum and adjacent loops of bowel, forming a pericecal abscess or phlegmon. May be managed initially with percutaneous drainage and antibiotics (interval appendectomy at 6-8 weeks) or by immediate surgery depending on severity.

Recurrent episodes of right iliac fossa pain with serological evidence of inflammation, resolving spontaneously and recurring months later. Represents a subset where incomplete obstruction and inflammation recur. Definitive treatment is interval appendectomy.

primarySymptoms: Periumbilical or central abdominal pain migrating to the right iliac fossa (McBurney's point) over 6-12 hours — the classic migratory pattern, present in 50-60% of cases, reflects the shift from visceral to somatic peritoneal pain as inflammation reaches the parietal peritoneum.
Right iliac fossa (RIF) tenderness on palpation, most intense at McBurney's point (one-third of the distance from the anterior superior iliac spine to the umbilicus), present in over 90% of cases.
Rebound tenderness — pain that worsens sharply when the examiner suddenly releases pressure on the right iliac fossa — indicating parietal peritoneal irritation.
Anorexia (loss of appetite), typically the first symptom before pain begins; found in approximately 90% of patients with confirmed appendicitis.
Nausea and vomiting developing after the onset of pain — vomiting before pain is more characteristic of gastroenteritis or bowel obstruction than appendicitis.
Low-grade fever (37.5-38.5°C) in most early cases; high fever above 38.5°C or rigors suggest perforation or established peritonitis.
Rovsing's sign — right iliac fossa pain elicited by palpation of the left iliac fossa — reflecting referred parietal peritoneal irritation on the right side.
Psoas sign — right iliac fossa pain worsened by passive extension of the right hip — indicates a retrocecal appendix irritating the iliopsoas muscle.
Guarding and rigidity of the right iliac fossa — involuntary muscle contraction protecting the inflamed peritoneum, ranging from mild voluntary guarding to frank board-like rigidity in perforation.
Constipation, occasionally alternating with diarrhea, from paralytic ileus and mesenteric irritation.
earlyWarningSigns: New onset of central abdominal pain in a young adult or child that persists more than 4-6 hours without resolution — the majority of appendicitis episodes declare themselves within this window.
Anorexia combined with right-sided or migrating abdominal pain, even mild, in a patient under 40 — warrants urgent clinical assessment.

White blood cell count above 11,000 cells/µL combined with C-reactive protein above 10 mg/L and right iliac fossa tenderness — a sensitive laboratory combination supporting appendicitis before imaging.
A child or adolescent presenting to the emergency department with repeated episodes of right iliac fossa pain and fever — does not resolve spontaneously and requires assessment for appendicitis.

Any pregnant woman with right-sided abdominal pain — the appendix migrates superolaterally in pregnancy, and appendicitis in pregnancy carries significantly higher fetal and maternal mortality than in non-pregnant patients.
emergencySigns: Sudden cessation of pain followed minutes to hours later by generalized severe abdominal pain and rigidity — the classic sequence of appendiceal perforation; requires emergency surgery.
Generalized abdominal tenderness, guarding, and boardlike rigidity — signs of generalized peritonitis requiring immediate surgical consultation and IV antibiotics.
Fever above 38.5°C, tachycardia, hypotension, and abdominal distension — signs of sepsis from perforated appendicitis; fluid resuscitation and emergency surgery are required.
Visible or palpable right iliac fossa mass combined with high fever and leukocytosis — suggests appendiceal phlegmon or abscess; urgent imaging and surgical or interventional radiology assessment.
Acute abdominal pain in a child who refuses to walk or lies still with both hips and knees flexed — characteristic protective posture in peritonitis; do not delay surgical assessment.

causes.description: An inspissated fecal concretion (calcified or non-calcified) lodges in the appendiceal orifice or lumen in approximately 30-35% of appendicitis cases, particularly in adults with complicated appendicitis. Fecaliths are visible on CT as hyper-dense intraluminal densities and are associated with higher rates of perforation because they cause complete obstruction.

The appendiceal submucosa contains abundant lymphoid follicles that enlarge substantially in response to viral gastroenteritis (adenovirus, rotavirus, Yersinia), mesenteric adenitis, or inflammatory bowel disease, compressing the lumen from within. This mechanism predominates in children and adolescents.

Carcinoid tumors at the appendiceal tip, mucoceles, or cecal carcinoma extending into the appendiceal orifice can cause luminal obstruction. Carcinoid is the most common appendiceal tumor and is found incidentally in approximately 0.3% of appendectomies; tumors under 2 cm are cured by appendectomy alone.

Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) lodges in the appendiceal lumen in endemic areas and is found in approximately 1-3% of appendectomies in low-income countries. Ascaris lumbricoides and other intestinal nematodes can similarly obstruct the appendiceal orifice.

Once obstruction occurs, normal gut flora — including Escherichia coli, Bacteroides fragilis, Klebsiella, and Enterococcus — proliferate rapidly in the obstructed lumen. Bacterial mucolysis breaches the appendiceal epithelium, allowing invasion of the wall and progressive transmural inflammation within 8-16 hours.

riskFactors.description: Appendicitis peaks in adolescence and young adulthood when lymphoid tissue in the appendix is most hyperplastic and reactive to enteric infections. Incidence per 100,000 is highest in the 10-19 year age group in all epidemiological series.

Males have approximately 1.4-fold higher lifetime risk than females, though the sex difference narrows with age. The higher negative appendectomy rate in young women reflects overlapping gynecological pathology (ovarian cysts, ectopic pregnancy) that mimics appendicitis.
First-degree relatives of appendicitis patients have approximately 3-fold higher risk, suggesting a genetic predisposition to appendiceal anatomy, fecalith formation, or immune response patterns.

Epidemiological studies show lower appendicitis rates in populations with high fiber diets, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa. Low fiber causes harder, larger-diameter stools that may be more likely to form fecaliths. The effect size is modest and confounded by other dietary and infectious factors.

Episodes of viral gastroenteritis and mesenteric adenitis — particularly adenovirus and Yersinia enterocolitica — trigger lymphoid hyperplasia in the appendiceal submucosa and temporally cluster with appendicitis cases in community outbreaks.
Direct abdominal trauma can predispose to appendicitis through intramural hematoma formation or secondary inflammation, though this is an uncommon mechanism compared with luminal obstruction.

riskFactors.factor: Extremes of age when appendicitis occurs
riskFactors.description: Children under 5 and adults over 65 experience perforation rates of 50-75% and 50-70% respectively — substantially higher than in young adults — because of delayed presentation, atypical symptoms, and higher diagnostic difficulty.

affectedDemographics: Adolescents and young adults (ages 10-30) have the highest incidence; peak in males ages 10-19 and females ages 10-19.
Males have a higher lifetime risk (approximately 8.6%) than females (6.7%), but females ages 20-40 have higher rates of diagnostic uncertainty from gynecological differential diagnoses.
Children under 5 and elderly patients have atypical presentations, late diagnoses, and perforation rates up to 50-75%, accounting for disproportionate morbidity.
Global variation is significant: appendicitis was historically rare in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia but rates are rising with westernization of diet, parallel to global trends.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of acute appendicitis combines clinical assessment, laboratory markers, and imaging. The Alvarado score (0-10) assigns points for migratory pain, anorexia, nausea/vomiting, right iliac fossa tenderness, rebound tenderness, elevated temperature, leukocytosis, and neutrophilia; a score of 7 or above has approximately 81% sensitivity and 74% specificity for appendicitis. The Appendicitis Inflammatory Response (AIR) score incorporates vomiting, right iliac fossa pain, rebound tenderness, guarding, CRP, WBC, and polymorphonuclear leukocytes — validation studies show superior performance to the Alvarado score in stratifying low-, medium-, and high-risk groups. Laboratory investigations include a full blood count (leukocytosis with neutrophilia in approximately 80% of cases), C-reactive protein (typically above 10-20 mg/L in confirmed appendicitis; highly elevated CRP above 100 mg/L correlates with perforation), and urinalysis to exclude UTI. Urine or serum beta-hCG is mandatory in any woman of reproductive age to exclude ectopic pregnancy. CT abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast has replaced plain X-ray as the imaging standard: sensitivity 94-98%, specificity 95-98% for appendicitis; findings include an appendiceal diameter above 6 mm, periappendiceal fat stranding, an obstructing appendicolith, and pericecal fluid. Ultrasound is first-line in children, pregnant women, and young women of reproductive age to avoid radiation; it is operator-dependent with sensitivity of 71-90% and specificity 88-97% in expert hands. MRI is increasingly used in pregnant women when ultrasound is equivocal, with sensitivity above 96%. Diagnostic laparoscopy remains the ultimate definitive test when imaging and clinical assessment are inconclusive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies leukocytosis (WBC >11,000/µL) and neutrophilia supporting appendicitis; also stratifies risk of perforation when combined with CRP
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 30-60 minutes. WBC is elevated in approximately 80% of appendicitis cases. Normal WBC does not exclude appendicitis, especially early.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A sensitive acute-phase reactant that rises over 8-12 hours; combined with WBC improves diagnostic accuracy; CRP above 100 mg/L predicts perforation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; result in 1-2 hours. CRP is normal in the very early phase (first 4-6 hours) but rises rapidly. Serial CRP at 8 and 24 hours improves sensitivity.

diagnosticTests.test: CT abdomen and pelvis (with IV contrast)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive imaging for appendicitis — identifies appendiceal wall thickening, luminal dilation, periappendiceal fat stranding, appendicolith, and complications
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Contrast dye injected via IV. Scan takes 10 minutes. Radiation dose approximately 8-10 mSv. Results interpreted by radiologist within 30-60 minutes. Gold standard for diagnostic accuracy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging in children, pregnant women, and young women to avoid radiation; visualizes an inflamed non-compressible appendix >6 mm diameter
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel applied to the abdomen; 15-20 minutes. Operator-dependent; a non-visualized appendix (negative by default) is common and does not exclude appendicitis. Sensitivity 71-90%.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Structured clinical decision score stratifying low-, medium-, and high-risk appendicitis for imaging and management decisions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: No invasive test — the clinician scores symptoms and lab values. Low-risk patients (Alvarado 1-4) may be observed without imaging; high-risk patients (Alvarado 7-10) proceed to surgery or confirmatory imaging.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory in all women of reproductive age to exclude ectopic pregnancy before appendicitis workup proceeds
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urine sample result in 2-5 minutes. A positive hCG in a woman with right lower abdominal pain fundamentally changes the differential diagnosis and management pathway.

treatmentOverview: For decades, appendectomy has been the standard-of-care for all cases of acute appendicitis. The introduction of laparoscopic appendectomy in 1983 (Semm) revolutionized surgical management: laparoscopic approach is now used in more than 90% of cases in high-income countries, with a wound infection rate below 3%, hospital stay of 1-2 days, and return to normal activity in 1-2 weeks compared with 2-4 weeks for open surgery. The World Society of Emergency Surgery (WSES) 2020 guidelines recommend laparoscopic appendectomy as the surgical standard. Non-operative management with antibiotics alone for uncomplicated (non-perforated) appendicitis has been established by multiple randomized trials — the APPAC (2015, 2021 follow-up) and CODA (2020) trials demonstrated that approximately 73% of patients treated with antibiotics avoided surgery at 5 years, but approximately 27% had recurrent appendicitis requiring delayed appendectomy. Antibiotic-first management is a clinically valid alternative for uncomplicated appendicitis in appropriately counseled patients who strongly prefer to avoid surgery and accept the approximately 25-40% recurrence risk. It is not appropriate for complicated appendicitis (perforation, abscess, phlegmon), suspected fecalith obstruction, or children with uncertain follow-up. For complicated appendicitis with peritonitis, emergency laparoscopic or open appendectomy with peritoneal lavage and IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or metronidazole plus a third-generation cephalosporin) is the standard. Appendiceal abscess or phlegmon without generalized peritonitis can be managed initially with IV antibiotics and CT-guided percutaneous drainage (if technically feasible) followed by interval appendectomy at 6-8 weeks. Perioperative antibiotics covering Gram-negative bacilli and anaerobes (cefoxitin or co-amoxiclav as a single prophylactic dose for uncomplicated cases) reduce wound infection risk.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic-first non-operative management (uncomplicated appendicitis only)
medicalTreatments.description: IV ertapenem or ceftriaxone plus metronidazole for 2-4 days followed by oral antibiotics (co-amoxiclav or ciprofloxacin plus metronidazole) for 7-10 days. Requires hospitalization for 24-48 hours initially, serial clinical reassessment, and CT confirmation of non-complicated disease without appendicolith.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: APPAC 2021 5-year follow-up: 73% appendectomy-free; CODA 2020: 71% avoided surgery at 30 days. Recurrence risk approximately 25-40% over 5 years. Not appropriate for complicated appendicitis.

medicalTreatments.name: Perioperative IV antibiotics (single dose prophylaxis for surgery)
medicalTreatments.description: Cefoxitin 2 g IV or co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV given within 60 minutes before skin incision. Reduces post-appendectomy wound infection from approximately 15% to below 3%. Extended postoperative antibiotics are not needed for uncomplicated appendicitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane 2014: perioperative antibiotics reduce wound infection risk in appendicitis surgery by 50-70%; NNT approximately 10 for uncomplicated, 5 for complicated appendicitis

medicalTreatments.name: IV antibiotics for complicated appendicitis (piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g every 8 hours, or meropenem in severe sepsis)
medicalTreatments.description: Following surgery for complicated appendicitis with perforation or abscess, IV antibiotics are continued for 3-5 days (or until fever and leukocytosis resolve) to treat established peritoneal contamination. Duration determined by clinical and laboratory response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IV antibiotics plus surgery: 30-day mortality below 2% in uncomplicated peritonitis from appendicitis; mortality rises to 5-15% with delayed presentation and feculent peritonitis

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesics — IV opioids (morphine 2-5 mg IV or fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV) and NSAIDs
medicalTreatments.description: Early adequate analgesia does not obscure the clinical examination — multiple randomized trials confirm that opioid analgesia in suspected appendicitis reduces patient distress without increasing diagnostic error. Withholding analgesia is no longer justified.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Ranji et al. 2006 meta-analysis: adequate opioid analgesia does not significantly alter diagnostic accuracy or alter surgical decision in acute abdominal pain

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic appendectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Three-port laparoscopic approach under general anaesthesia: appendix identified, mesoappendix ligated, appendiceal base divided with endoscopic stapler or loop ligature, specimen retrieved in a bag. Preferred approach for most patients including pregnant women after 20 weeks and obese patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Wound infection rate <3%; conversion to open <5%; hospital stay 1-2 days; 30-day complication rate 4-6%; overall success rate >97%

surgicalOptions.name: Open appendectomy (McBurney/grid-iron incision)
surgicalOptions.description: Traditional open approach via a right iliac fossa incision, still used in cases with extensive peritonitis, when laparoscopic equipment is unavailable, or in resource-limited settings. Recovery is somewhat longer but outcomes are comparable in experienced hands.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mortality below 0.1% for uncomplicated appendicitis; wound infection rate approximately 5-10%; hospital stay 2-4 days

surgicalOptions.name: CT-guided percutaneous drainage of appendiceal abscess
surgicalOptions.description: Image-guided catheter drainage of a localized pericecal abscess in haemodynamically stable patients, followed by IV antibiotics and interval appendectomy at 6-8 weeks. Avoids emergency surgery through an inflamed, poorly demarcated operative field.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success >90%; clinical resolution in 70-85% allowing interval appendectomy; failure rates requiring emergency surgery approximately 10-15%

whySeeSpecialist: Any patient with suspected appendicitis requires immediate surgical assessment in an emergency department or by a general surgeon. Delay increases perforation risk, which doubles mortality and significantly increases morbidity. For complicated appendicitis (abscess, phlegmon), interventional radiology for percutaneous drainage and surgical planning are both needed.
doctorSelectionGuide: Emergency appendectomy should be performed by a general surgeon trained in laparoscopy at a hospital with 24-hour surgical capability. For interval or elective appendectomy, a minimally invasive surgeon with high laparoscopic volume achieves the lowest complication rates. For appendicitis with suspicion of underlying bowel disease or tumor, colorectal surgical review is appropriate.
hospitalCriteria: 24/7 general surgery availability with laparoscopic capability
CT scanning available around the clock with urgent radiological reporting
ICU or high-dependency unit for complicated appendicitis with sepsis or multi-organ involvement
Interventional radiology for percutaneous abscess drainage in selected cases
Pediatric surgical capability for children under 16 with appendicitis
keyFacilities: Emergency departments at hospitals with 24/7 surgical coverage
General surgery departments at district general and teaching hospitals
Pediatric surgery centers for children and adolescents
Minimally invasive surgery units with experienced laparoscopic surgeons
preventionStrategies: Maintain adequate dietary fiber intake (25-38 g/day) from vegetables, fruits, legumes, and whole grains — epidemiological data associates high-fiber diets with lower appendicitis rates, though the relationship is not fully established.
Stay hydrated to promote regular bowel motility and reduce fecalith formation from inspissated stool.
Seek prompt medical evaluation for right iliac fossa pain lasting more than 4-6 hours — early diagnosis prevents perforation and its associated complications.
Manage gastrointestinal infections promptly: Yersinia enterocolitica and adenoviral gastroenteritis are recognized triggers for lymphoid hyperplasia leading to appendicitis.

For patients with a history of recurrent appendicitis (recurrent RIF pain and elevated inflammatory markers), elective interval appendectomy should be discussed with a surgeon before a further acute episode occurs.
lifestyleModifications: Post-appendectomy: avoid lifting more than 5-10 kg for 2 weeks after laparoscopic appendectomy to allow port-site healing and reduce hernia risk.
Resume normal diet gradually after surgery — start with clear fluids and advance to solid food as bowel sounds return, typically within 24 hours of laparoscopic surgery.
Monitor the wound site for signs of infection (increasing redness, swelling, discharge, or fever) in the first 2 weeks after surgery.
Avoid vigorous abdominal exercise for 4-6 weeks after appendectomy; walking from day 1 post-surgery is encouraged to reduce VTE risk.

If treated non-operatively with antibiotics, attend all scheduled follow-up appointments and return immediately if symptoms recur — approximately 25-40% will develop recurrent appendicitis within 5 years.
Maintain adequate hydration and fiber intake as part of long-term gastrointestinal health following recovery.

recommended: High-fiber foods (vegetables, fruits, legumes, whole grains) targeting 25-38 g of fiber daily for general colorectal health and potentially reducing fecalith formation
Adequate hydration (1.5-2.5 L per day) to support normal bowel transit
Clear fluids and easily digested foods in the immediate 24-48 hours post-surgery
Probiotic foods (yogurt, kefir) during and after antibiotic treatment to restore gut microbiome balance
avoid: Highly processed, low-fiber foods that contribute to constipation and harder stools
Heavy fatty meals in the first week after appendectomy — may increase post-operative nausea
Alcohol for at least 48 hours post-surgery due to interaction with anesthesia and analgesics
No specific long-term dietary restrictions after uncomplicated appendectomy

exerciseGuidelines: Walking from the day after laparoscopic appendectomy is encouraged and reduces VTE risk. Light daily activities can be resumed within 1 week. Driving is permitted when the patient can perform an emergency brake without pain, typically 1-2 weeks post-surgery. Return to full activity, sport, and abdominal exercises at 4-6 weeks after uncomplicated laparoscopic appendectomy.
dailyManagement: After non-operative management: monitor temperature twice daily and document any recurrence of RIF pain, fever, or GI symptoms — report immediately to the treating team.
After surgery: take analgesics (paracetamol, ibuprofen) at scheduled intervals for the first 3-5 days to maintain pain control and facilitate mobilization.
Keep wound dressings dry for 48 hours; after that, showering is permissible — avoid submersion in baths or pools until wounds are fully healed.
Complete the full oral antibiotic course if prescribed post-operatively for complicated appendicitis — do not stop early even if symptoms resolve.
Attend GP or surgeon review at 10-14 days after discharge to check wound healing and review histopathology of the removed appendix.
If stool has not been passed within 72 hours after surgery, contact the surgical team — constipation post-appendectomy is common and can be managed with gentle laxatives.
prognosis: Uncomplicated acute appendicitis treated by laparoscopic appendectomy carries a 30-day mortality below 0.1%, wound infection rate under 3%, and return to full activity within 1-2 weeks. Complicated appendicitis with perforation and generalized peritonitis in a previously healthy adult has a 30-day mortality of approximately 2-5%, rising to 10-15% in the elderly or in delayed presentations. Post-appendectomy intra-abdominal abscess occurs in approximately 2-5% of perforated cases. Non-operative antibiotic management avoids surgery in approximately 73% of patients over 5 years; the remaining 27% require delayed appendectomy, mostly without complications. Appendiceal carcinoid tumors less than 2 cm — found in 0.3% of appendectomy specimens — are cured by appendectomy alone with essentially 100% 5-year survival. Long-term bowel function is normal after appendectomy; concerns about increased colorectal cancer risk after appendectomy are not supported by current evidence.
recoveryTimeline: Laparoscopic appendectomy: discharge day 1-2; return to light work and daily activities 1-2 weeks; full recovery including sports 4-6 weeks. Complicated appendicitis: hospital stay 4-7 days; return to work 3-4 weeks; full recovery 6-8 weeks. Non-operative management: hospital observation 24-48 hours; complete the 10-day antibiotic course; return to normal activities within 1-2 weeks; follow-up at 4-6 weeks.
complications: Perforation and feculent peritonitis — occurs in approximately 20-30% of all appendicitis presentations overall, rising to 50-75% in children under 5 and elderly adults due to diagnostic delay; increases mortality and hospital stay by 3-5 fold.
Intra-abdominal abscess (pelvic, subphrenic, or RIF) — develops in 2-5% of appendectomy cases, particularly after perforated appendicitis; treated with CT-guided drainage and antibiotics.
Wound infection — superficial site infection in approximately 3-5% of laparoscopic appendectomies, 10-15% of open; treated with wound opening and dressing changes; rarely requires re-admission.
Stump appendicitis — rare recurrence of appendicitis from a long residual appendiceal stump left at initial appendectomy; presents months to years later with identical symptoms.
Small bowel obstruction from adhesions — long-term risk of approximately 2-5% after appendicitis, particularly after open surgery or complicated appendicitis with peritonitis.
Missed diagnosis leading to sepsis — failure to diagnose appendicitis in elderly, immunocompromised, or atypically presenting patients leads to perforation, sepsis, and potentially fatal peritonitis.

supportResources.name: American College of Surgeons — Patient Education: Appendectomy
supportResources.description: Surgeon-reviewed patient guide to appendicitis, surgery, and post-operative recovery.

supportResources.name: NHS — Appendicitis
supportResources.description: NHS patient information covering symptoms, diagnosis, emergency surgery, and recovery.

supportResources.name: World Society of Emergency Surgery (WSES) — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Professional society for emergency surgery with published guidelines on appendicitis management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Liver abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Liver abscess causes right upper quadrant rather than right iliac fossa pain, fever, and elevated liver enzymes — contrasting with the typical McBurney's point tenderness of appendicitis. CT distinguishes the two clearly: hepatic abscess appears as a hypo-dense liver collection, while appendicitis shows periappendiceal inflammation at the RIF.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute cholecystitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cholecystitis causes right upper quadrant pain radiating to the right shoulder tip and positive Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest during RUQ palpation), unlike the right iliac fossa localization of appendicitis. Ultrasound differentiates gallbladder inflammation with gallstones from a normal gallbladder with RIF periappendiceal changes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS causes recurrent abdominal pain relieved by defecation, bloating, and altered stool form without fever or leukocytosis. Appendicitis pain does not resolve with defecation, progresses over hours, and is accompanied by objective signs of inflammation. IBS is a chronic functional diagnosis — new, persistent, worsening RIF pain with fever should always prompt appendicitis evaluation.

relatedConditions.name: Acute cholecystitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are acute abdominal surgical emergencies requiring urgent assessment; cholecystitis is in the differential for right-sided abdominal pain.

relatedConditions.name: Colorectal cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Cecal carcinoma can present as acute appendicitis through obstruction of the appendiceal orifice; histopathology of appendectomy specimens occasionally reveals an underlying tumor.

relatedConditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: IBS causes recurrent right iliac fossa pain that is frequently confused with appendicitis; distinguishing features include chronicity, absence of fever, and normal inflammatory markers in IBS.

relatedConditions.name: Peptic ulcer
relatedConditions.relevance: Perforated peptic ulcer can cause free air tracking to the right paracolic gutter mimicking appendicitis; erect chest X-ray showing free air distinguishes the two.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of appendicitis?
faqs.answer: The first sign is typically loss of appetite followed by central or umbilical abdominal pain that gradually migrates to the right iliac fossa over 6-12 hours. Nausea and a low-grade fever develop as inflammation progresses. The migration of pain to the right lower abdomen in a patient with anorexia is the classic diagnostic sequence.

faqs.question: Where is appendicitis pain located?
faqs.answer: Pain begins centrally around the navel and moves to McBurney's point — one-third of the distance from the right anterior superior iliac spine to the umbilicus — over 6-12 hours. This migration occurs in approximately 50-60% of cases. In retrocecal appendix, pain may be more posterior or flank-based.

faqs.question: Can appendicitis go away without surgery?
faqs.answer: For uncomplicated (non-perforated) appendicitis, antibiotic treatment alone avoids surgery in approximately 73% of patients over 5 years according to the APPAC and CODA trials. However, about 27% develop recurrent appendicitis requiring delayed surgery. Perforated or gangrenous appendicitis requires emergency surgery.

faqs.question: How is appendicitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Appendicitis is diagnosed from clinical signs (right iliac fossa tenderness, rebound), blood tests showing raised white cells and CRP, and imaging. CT scan is the most accurate test (sensitivity 94-98%). Ultrasound is used first in children and pregnant women to avoid radiation. The Alvarado or AIR score guides the decision to image.

faqs.question: What happens if appendicitis is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated appendicitis progresses to gangrene and perforation within 24-72 hours of symptom onset in most cases. Perforation releases bowel bacteria into the abdomen causing peritonitis, sepsis, and potentially death. Perforation rates reach 50-75% in children under 5 and elderly adults, where diagnosis is typically delayed.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after appendectomy?
faqs.answer: After laparoscopic appendectomy, most patients are discharged within 1-2 days and return to light activities within 1-2 weeks. Full recovery including resuming exercise takes 4-6 weeks. Complicated appendicitis requiring open surgery or treating peritonitis may require 6-8 weeks for full recovery.

faqs.question: Is appendicitis a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Appendicitis is an acute surgical emergency. Any delay in diagnosis and treatment increases the risk of perforation, peritonitis, and sepsis. Patients with suspected appendicitis should be assessed urgently in an emergency department — do not wait to see if the pain resolves on its own beyond a few hours.

faqs.question: What is the appendix and why does it get inflamed?
faqs.answer: The appendix is a 6-10 cm finger-like pouch attached to the large bowel in the right lower abdomen. It becomes inflamed when its opening gets blocked — most commonly by a hardened piece of stool (fecalith) or by enlarged lymph nodes following a gut infection — allowing bacteria to proliferate inside and infect the appendix wall.

faqs.question: Can you have appendicitis without fever?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fever is absent in approximately 20-30% of appendicitis cases, especially early in the illness or in elderly patients. Normal temperature does not rule out appendicitis. The combination of right iliac fossa tenderness, anorexia, and elevated CRP without fever still warrants urgent evaluation and imaging.

faqs.question: What blood tests show appendicitis?
faqs.answer: There is no single definitive blood test. Appendicitis is supported by a white blood cell count above 11,000 per microlitre and CRP above 10-20 mg/L, but both can be normal early in the illness. The combination of leukocytosis, elevated CRP, and right iliac fossa tenderness has approximately 90% sensitivity for appendicitis when all three are present.

faqs.question: Is appendicitis more common in children?
faqs.answer: Appendicitis peaks in childhood and young adulthood (ages 10-30) but can occur at any age. Children under 5 have high rates of diagnostic delay and perforation (up to 75%) because symptoms are less typical. Children with appendicitis often present with diffuse abdominal pain, vomiting, and inability to walk rather than the classic right lower quadrant localization.

faqs.question: Can appendicitis recur after antibiotic treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 25-40% of patients treated non-operatively with antibiotics develop recurrent appendicitis within 5 years, most within the first year. Patients choosing antibiotic-first management must understand and accept this recurrence risk and have a clear plan for prompt re-evaluation if symptoms return.

faqs.question: What is the difference between appendicitis and ovarian cyst pain?
faqs.answer: Both cause right lower abdominal pain, but ovarian cyst or ectopic pregnancy occur in women of reproductive age and are distinguished by a positive pregnancy test, pelvic ultrasound showing ovarian pathology, and often a more sudden onset of pain. Appendicitis causes fever and rising inflammatory markers; ovarian cysts typically do not unless ruptured and infected.

faqs.question: How long does appendicitis take to perforate?
faqs.answer: Perforation typically occurs 24-72 hours after symptom onset, though this is highly variable. Some appendices perforate within 12-18 hours in children under 5 with rapid progression; others show slow walling-off into a phlegmon or abscess over 4-5 days without free perforation.

faqs.question: Does diet affect the risk of appendicitis?
faqs.answer: A low-fiber diet is associated with higher appendicitis rates in epidemiological studies, possibly by promoting fecalith formation. Populations with high fiber intake (25-38 g/day) from vegetables, fruits, and legumes have lower reported appendicitis rates. However, the relationship is observational and diet alone cannot fully prevent appendicitis.
